Front: PyTorch: what's the actual difference between what bias does and what an activation function does?
Back: Bias (the +b in Wx+b) shifts a linear function so it doesn't have to pass through the origin, independent of activation functions. Stacking multiple linear layers with bias but NO activation still collapses algebraically to one linear function (W2(W1x+b1)+b2 = (W2W1)x + (W2b1+b2)). Activation functions (ReLU etc.) are what make depth meaningful: without non-linearity between layers, any number of stacked layers has the same representational power as a single layer.

Front: PyTorch tensor broadcasting: state the actual compatibility rule (not just 'one side must be 1').
Back: Compare shapes dimension-by-dimension, aligned from the trailing (rightmost) dimension. Two dimensions are compatible if they're equal or one of them is 1; missing leading dimensions are treated as 1. Example: (3,2) broadcasts with (2,) [trailing dims 2 and 2 match] but NOT with (3,) [trailing dims 2 vs 3, neither is 1, no alignment].

Front: What does a PyTorch tensor give you over a plain Python list that specifically ENABLES training a neural network (not just makes it faster/more convenient)?
Back: Autograd (gradient tracking). backward() computes d(loss)/d(param) for every parameter via the chain rule (reverse-mode autodiff), which gradient descent needs to know how to update each weight. GPU speed and torch.nn ecosystem integration make training fast and convenient, but without gradient tracking there's no mechanism to compute weight updates at all - training wouldn't be possible regardless of speed.

Front: Meta-lesson: when explaining why a tool/concept matters, what's the failure pattern to watch for?
Back: Reaching for the most visible or impressive-sounding advantage (GPU speed, ecosystem integration) instead of the actual load-bearing mechanism (autograd, for tensors specifically). Convenience reasons are real but secondary to the reason the thing is possible at all.
